citation.sourceDocument: South Carolina H.4216 (2026), S.C. Code 12-6-510 rates & 12-6-1140(15) SCIAD; SCDOR 'Information about H. 4216'
citation.sourceNote: H.4216 (signed by Gov. McMaster on 2026-03-30, effective tax year 2026) restructured South Carolina's individual income tax into TWO brackets — 1.99% on the first $30,000 of South Carolina taxable income and 5.21% above $30,000 (no 0% bracket) — uniform across every filing status (S.C. Code §12-6-510). The reform decoupled from the federal deductions: federal adjusted gross income is now the starting point, and the standard deduction is the new South Carolina Income Adjusted Deduction (SCIAD, §12-6-1140(15)) of $15,000 (single / married filing separately), $22,500 (head of household), and $30,000 (married filing jointly / surviving spouse). The SCIAD is a SLIDING deduction: it is reduced by the fraction (federal AGI − threshold) ÷ divisor, where the threshold/divisor are $40,000/$55,000 (single, fully gone by $95,000 AGI), $60,000/$82,500 (head of household, gone by $142,500), and $80,000/$110,000 (joint, gone by $190,000); per §12-6-1140(15)(c) the reduction is rounded DOWN to the next-lowest $10. Modeled via the engine's optional `standardDeductionPhaseOut` capability — the sliding-deduction form the roadmap had deferred. Worked example, single $60,000 AGI: reduction = $15,000 × (60,000 − 40,000)/55,000 = $5,454.54 → rounded down to $5,450, so SCIAD = $9,550 and taxable = $50,450. The dependent exemption and other SC credits are omitted at launch fidelity. Verified against the SCDOR 'Information about H. 4216' page and the H.4216 bill text (§12-6-1140(15)), cross-checked against the Tax Foundation 2026 table, on 2026-06-06.
